questions.q: What is the exact research question in one sentence?
questions.good: Does instruction tuning with synthetic rationales improve factual grounding in low-resource QA compared to standard fine-tuning?
questions.fix: Add task, data, intervention, baseline, metric

questions.q: Can the problem be stated without naming a model?
questions.good: Can adding chain-of-thought rationales to training data reduce hallucination in factual QA?
questions.fix: If the problem disappears when you remove the model name, it is not a research problem

questions.q: What is the smallest baseline that would embarrass your method if it performed equally well?
questions.good: TF-IDF + logistic regression on the same data. If it matches my LLM, my approach is not justified.
questions.fix: Define at least 3 baselines: trivial, classical, frozen LLM

questions.q: What evidence would make you abandon this direction?
questions.good: If a frozen LLM with good prompting matches my fine-tuned model on the target metric, the direction is dead.
questions.bad: Nothing, I believe in this direction
questions.fix: Define falsification criteria before starting

questions.q: Why is this an LLM problem rather than classical ML, rules, retrieval, or data cleaning?
questions.good: Because the output space is open-ended generation and the task requires world knowledge that cannot be hand-coded.
questions.fix: Name the specific capability only LLMs provide

questions.q: What is new: data, task, model, training method, evaluation, analysis, or application?
questions.good: New evaluation protocol that reveals failure modes existing metrics miss.
questions.fix: Pick one contribution type and defend it

questions.q: Who would care if the result is positive, and who would care if it is negative?
questions.good: Positive: QA system builders in low-resource domains. Negative: people claiming LLMs always need more data.
questions.fix: Name specific stakeholders

questions.q: What is one row/sample? Show a realistic example with fields.
questions.good: {"id": "q001", "question": "...", "context": "...", "answer": "...", "source": "wikipedia", "difficulty": "easy"}
questions.fix: Show the actual schema with realistic values

questions.q: What fields exist in raw data, and which are allowed at inference time?
questions.good: Raw has question, context, answer, annotator_id, source_url. Inference only gets question and context.
questions.fix: Separate metadata from inference-time features

questions.q: What is the label, and how was it created?
questions.good: Free-text answer extracted from context by 3 annotators with 0.82 inter-annotator agreement.
questions.bad: The labels are in the dataset
questions.fix: Describe the annotation process and reliability

questions.q: What information might leak from train to test?
questions.good: Same document passages appear in both splits. Same source URLs. Same annotator bias.
questions.fix: Check for duplicates, shared IDs, temporal leakage, metadata shortcuts

questions.q: What is the split strategy: random, stratified, temporal, group, domain-held-out, or source-held-out?
questions.good: Temporal split: train on pre-2023, test on post-2023. Group split by document ID to prevent passage leakage.
questions.fix: Explain why this split matches the deployment scenario

questions.q: What preprocessing could accidentally remove signal or introduce artifacts?
questions.good: Lowercasing removes named entity signal. Truncation at 512 tokens removes 15% of answers that appear late in context.
questions.fix: Audit each preprocessing step for signal loss and artifact creation

questions.q: What labels are ambiguous or noisy, and how will you measure that?
questions.good: 12% of answers have multiple valid phrasings. Measuring with exact match vs F1 vs human judgment.
questions.fix: Quantify label noise and choose metrics that handle it

questions.q: What data cannot be used because of licensing, privacy, or ethical constraints?
questions.good: Cannot redistribute annotator IDs. Cannot use data from source X due to license restriction.
questions.fix: Check licenses, PII, and ethical review requirements

questions.q: What framework are you using: raw PyTorch, Lightning, HF Trainer, Accelerate, DeepSpeed, FSDP, PEFT, or custom loop? Why?
questions.good: HF Trainer because it handles distributed training and gradient accumulation. Custom eval loop because Trainer's compute_metrics doesn't support my metric.
questions.fix: Match framework choice to failure mode: correctness/memory/speed/quality/reproducibility

questions.q: What exact tensor shapes enter the model? Show one post-collation batch.
questions.good: input_ids: [8, 512], attention_mask: [8, 512], labels: [8, 512] with -100 on prompt tokens.
questions.fix: Print batch keys and shapes before training

questions.q: Where are labels created, shifted, padded, and masked?
questions.good: Labels are shifted left by 1 in the model forward. Prompt tokens masked with -100 in data collator. Padding masked in loss function.
questions.fix: Trace label flow from data to loss

questions.q: Which parameters are trainable and how many?
questions.good: LoRA on q_proj and v_proj. 2.1M trainable out of 7B total (0.03%).
questions.fix: Print trainable parameter count and names

questions.q: Can you overfit 8 examples? If not, what is broken?
questions.good: Yes, loss collapses to 0.01 in 50 steps.
questions.fix: Run 8-example overfit test before any serious training

questions.q: What is the loss function, and does it match the task?
questions.good: CrossEntropyLoss for classification. Labels are integer class IDs. Loss ignores padding via ignore_index=-100.
questions.fix: Verify loss matches task type and label format

questions.q: What part of memory is dominant: weights, activations, optimizer states, gradients, KV cache, or dataloader?
questions.good: Activations dominate at seq_len=2048 with batch_size=4. Switching to gradient checkpointing reduces by 60%.
questions.fix: Profile memory breakdown before scaling up

questions.q: What checkpoint would let another person resume the exact run?
questions.good: checkpoint-1000/ contains model.safetensors, optimizer.pt, scheduler.pt, trainer_state.json, training_args.yaml, random seeds logged.
questions.fix: Save model + optimizer + scheduler + config + seeds

questions.q: What is the main claim of the paper/chapter in one sentence?
questions.good: Instruction tuning with chain-of-thought rationales improves factual grounding by 15% on TriviaQA.
questions.bad: The paper is about instruction tuning
questions.fix: Extract the falsifiable claim, not the topic

questions.q: What assumptions are required for that claim to hold?
questions.good: Assumes TriviaQA measures factual grounding. Assumes 15% improvement is not from data contamination. Assumes the rationale quality is sufficient.
questions.fix: List every assumption that could break the claim

questions.q: What experiment is the strongest evidence?
questions.good: Table 3: ablation removing rationales drops performance by 12%, showing rationales are the causal factor.
questions.fix: Name the single experiment that most supports the claim

questions.q: What baseline is suspiciously absent?
questions.good: No frozen LLM with good prompting. No retrieval-only baseline. No data augmentation without rationales.
questions.fix: Name at least one missing baseline

questions.q: What is reusable in your project: metric, dataset, architecture, training trick, evaluation protocol, or failure taxonomy?
questions.good: Their evaluation protocol with per-slice breakdown. Their failure taxonomy for hallucination types.
questions.fix: Pick specific reusable decisions, not general inspiration

questions.q: What would invalidate the claim on your dataset?
questions.good: If my dataset has different domain distribution, the rationale quality may not transfer. If my evaluation metric is stricter, the 15% gap may disappear.
questions.fix: Identify dataset/method/eval differences that could break transfer

questions.q: What is the hypothesis in if-then-because form?
questions.good: If we add chain-of-thought rationales to training data, then factual grounding improves by ≥10%, because rationales teach the model to reason before answering.
questions.bad: We will train a model and see
questions.fix: Use if-then-because format

questions.q: What is the control, intervention, metric, and stopping criterion?
questions.good: Control: standard fine-tuning. Intervention: + rationales. Metric: F1 on factual claims. Stop: when eval loss plateaus for 3 epochs.
questions.fix: Define all four explicitly

questions.q: What is the minimal ablation that tests your core claim?
questions.good: Remove rationales from training data, keep everything else identical. If performance drops, rationales are the cause.
questions.fix: Each ablation must remove one claimed source of improvement

questions.q: What result table would convince a skeptical reviewer?
questions.good: Table with: baseline, main run, ablation (no rationales), ablation (no instruction tuning), per-slice breakdown, 3 seeds with std dev.
questions.fix: Design the table before running experiments

questions.q: What error slices will you inspect after aggregate metrics?
questions.good: Length bins, question type (factual vs reasoning), domain (science vs history), difficulty (easy vs hard), hallucination rate per slice.
questions.fix: Define error slices before training

questions.q: What is the compute budget, and how does it constrain model/data size?
questions.good: 2x A100 for 48 hours. Fits 7B model with LoRA, batch 8, seq 512. Cannot do full fine-tuning or larger models.
questions.fix: Define hardware, time, and resulting constraints

questions.q: How many seeds or repeated runs are needed for the claim you want to make?
questions.good: 3 seeds for main result, report mean ± std. If std > 2%, need more seeds or larger effect size.
questions.fix: Define seed count and significance threshold

questions.q: Why this problem and not a nearby easier one?
questions.good: Because the easier version (pure classification) has been solved and the harder version (open-ended generation with grounding) has real-world impact.
questions.fix: Defend the problem choice with impact and novelty

questions.q: What is the contribution if the model does not beat the strongest baseline?
questions.good: The contribution is the evaluation protocol that reveals failure modes, not the model performance.
questions.fix: Define contribution types beyond metric improvement

questions.q: Why should anyone trust your labels?
questions.good: 3 annotators, 0.82 agreement, adjudication protocol documented, edge cases sampled and reviewed.
questions.fix: Document annotation process and reliability

questions.q: How do you know the model learned the intended signal?
questions.good: Attention analysis shows focus on relevant context. Error analysis shows failures on cases where context is misleading. Ablation confirms rationale dependency.
questions.fix: Go beyond metrics: attention, error analysis, ablations

questions.q: What is the biggest threat to validity?
questions.good: Data contamination: some test questions may appear in LLM pretraining data. Mitigation: use post-cutoff data and check for memorization.
questions.fix: Name the #1 threat and your mitigation

scores.2: partial; some pieces exist but cannot guide implementation
scores.3: workable; enough to run a small experiment with risk

verdicts.idea_fog: < 1.5 average. Need problem and dataset definition before coding.
verdicts.pre_proposal: 1.5-2.4. Have direction but lack evidence and design.
